infobox.value: (official bilingual wording; [German](/wiki/German_language "German language"): *Biel* [[biːl]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Standard_German "Help:IPA/Standard German") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:De-Biel.ogg "File:De-Biel.ogg"); [French](/wiki/French_language "French language"): *Bienne* [[bjɛn]](/wiki/Help:IPA/French "Help:IPA/French"); [Alemannic German](/wiki/Alemannic_German_language "Alemannic German language"): *Biu* [[biːy]](/wiki/International_Phonetic_Alphabet "International Phonetic Alphabet")) is a bilingual [city](/wiki/City "City") in the [canton of Bern](/wiki/Canton_of_Bern "Canton of Bern") in [Switzerland](/wiki/Switzerland "Switzerland"). With over 55,000 residents, it is the country's [tenth-largest city by population](/wiki/List_of_cities_in_Switzerland "List of cities in Switzerland"). The Biel urban area has a population of around 100,000 inhabitants.[[3]](#cite_note-3) Biel/Bienne is the capital of the [Biel/Bienne administrative district](/wiki/Biel/Bienne_(administrative_district) "Biel/Bienne (administrative district)"). The city has been an industrial and watchmaking heart of Switzerland since the 19th century. With world-famous watch brands such as [Rolex](/wiki/Rolex "Rolex"), [Omega](/wiki/Omega_SA "Omega SA") and [Swatch](/wiki/Swatch "Swatch") based in Biel/Bienne, the city is one of the main centres of the Swiss watch industry and is also referred to as the "world capital of watchmaking".[[4]](#cite_note-4)[[5]](#cite_note-5)

**Biel/Bienne** (official bilingual wording; [German](/wiki/German_language "German language"): *Biel* [[biːl]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Standard_German "Help:IPA/Standard German") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:De-Biel.ogg "File:De-Biel.ogg"); [French](/wiki/French_language "French language"): *Bienne* [[bjɛn]](/wiki/Help:IPA/French "Help:IPA/French"); [Alemannic German](/wiki/Alemannic_German_language "Alemannic German language"): *Biu* [[biːy]](/wiki/International_Phonetic_Alphabet "International Phonetic Alphabet")) is a bilingual [city](/wiki/City "City") in the [canton of Bern](/wiki/Canton_of_Bern "Canton of Bern") in [Switzerland](/wiki/Switzerland "Switzerland"). With over 55,000 residents, it is the country's [tenth-largest city by population](/wiki/List_of_cities_in_Switzerland "List of cities in Switzerland"). The Biel urban area has a population of around 100,000 inhabitants.[[3]](#cite_note-3) Biel/Bienne is the capital of the [Biel/Bienne administrative district](/wiki/Biel/Bienne_(administrative_district) "Biel/Bienne (administrative district)"). The city has been an industrial and watchmaking heart of Switzerland since the 19th century. With world-famous watch brands such as [Rolex](/wiki/Rolex "Rolex"), [Omega](/wiki/Omega_SA "Omega SA") and [Swatch](/wiki/Swatch "Swatch") based in Biel/Bienne, the city is one of the main centres of the Swiss watch industry and is also referred to as the "world capital of watchmaking".[[4]](#cite_note-4)[[5]](#cite_note-5)

Biel/Bienne lies on the language boundary between the [French](/wiki/French_language "French language")-speaking and [German](/wiki/German_language "German language")-speaking parts of Switzerland, and is [bilingual](/wiki/Bilingual "Bilingual") throughout. *Biel* is the German name for the city whereas *Bienne* is its French counterpart. The city is often referred to in both languages simultaneously. Since 1 January 2005, the official name has been "Biel/Bienne". Until then, the town was officially named **Biel** or **Bienne**.[[6]](#cite_note-AGV-6)

The city lies at the foot of the first mountain range of the [Jura Mountains](/wiki/Jura_Mountains "Jura Mountains") area, guarding the only practical connection to Jura, on the northeastern shores of [Lake Biel](/wiki/Lake_Biel "Lake Biel") (*Bielersee*, *Lac de Bienne*), sharing the eastern tip of the lake with its sister town, [Nidau](/wiki/Nidau "Nidau"). The cities of [Neuchâtel](/wiki/Neuch%C3%A2tel "Neuchâtel"), [Solothurn](/wiki/Solothurn "Solothurn"), and [Bern](/wiki/Bern "Bern") (the [capital](/wiki/Capital_(political) "Capital (political)") of Switzerland) lie southwest, northeast and southeast of Biel/Bienne. They all can be reached within about 30 minutes by train or car. The cities of [Zurich](/wiki/Z%C3%BCrich "Zürich"), [Basel](/wiki/Basel "Basel") and [Lausanne](/wiki/Lausanne "Lausanne") can each be reached in about one hour by car or train.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Biel/Bienne&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

### Prehistoric settlements

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Biel/Bienne&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Prehistoric settlements")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/35/Biel_um_1546.jpg/250px-Biel_um_1546.jpg)](/wiki/File:Biel_um_1546.jpg)

Biel/Bienne in 1546

The shoreline of Lake Biel has been inhabited since at least the [Neolithic](/wiki/Neolithic "Neolithic") age. The remains of two neolithic settlements were found at [Vingelz](/wiki/Vinelz "Vinelz") in 1874. The remains of the settlements became the Vingelz / Hafen archaeological site, which is now part of a [UNESCO World Heritage Site](/wiki/UNESCO_World_Heritage_Site "UNESCO World Heritage Site"). East of the Vingelz site, a late [Bronze Age](/wiki/Bronze_Age "Bronze Age") settlement was also discovered.[[7]](#cite_note-HDS_Vingelz-7) After the [Roman conquest](/wiki/Gallic_Wars "Gallic Wars"), the region was part of [Germania Superior](/wiki/Germania_Superior "Germania Superior"). During the [Roman era](/wiki/Switzerland_in_the_Roman_era "Switzerland in the Roman era") the [Roman road](/wiki/Roman_road "Roman road") from [Petinesca](/wiki/Petinesca "Petinesca") to [Pierre Pertuis](/wiki/Pierre_Pertuis "Pierre Pertuis") or Salodurum (now [Solothurn](/wiki/Solothurn "Solothurn")) passed through the village of Mett, which is now part of Biel/Bienne. The foundations of buildings and a 4th-century cemetery in Mett come from a late Roman or an [early medieval](/wiki/Early_medieval "Early medieval") military guard station.[[8]](#cite_note-HDS_Mett-8)

A theory holds that the toponym is derived from the name of [Belenus](/wiki/Belenus "Belenus"), probably from a Roman era sanctuary of that deity at a [sacred spring](/wiki/Sacred_spring "Sacred spring") nearby. However, no surviving records or inscriptions confirm this theory. Another theory states that the town grew up around a late Roman fortress. While no trace of the fortress has been found, the foundations of several Roman buildings have been found east of the medieval town.[[9]](#cite_note-HDS-9)

The town is mentioned in 1142 as *Apud Belnam*,[[9]](#cite_note-HDS-9) which is taken as evidence for its derivation from *Belenus*. In [popular etymology](/wiki/Popular_etymology "Popular etymology"), the name has been connected with the German name for *axe* ([Bernese German](/wiki/Bernese_German "Bernese German") *bieli*), reflected in the two crossed axes in the town's coat of arms.

### Foundation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Biel/Bienne&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Foundation")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/63/Merian_Biel_1642.jpg/250px-Merian_Biel_1642.jpg)](/wiki/File:Merian_Biel_1642.jpg)

Biel/Bienne in 1642

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/76/Biel.jpg/250px-Biel.jpg)](/wiki/File:Biel.jpg)

[St Benedict's City Church](/wiki/City_Church_of_Biel "City Church of Biel") is one of the most important late-gothic buildings in Switzerland

In the 5th century, the area was invaded by the [Burgundians](/wiki/Burgundians "Burgundians"), and by the medieval period became part of [Upper Burgundy](/wiki/Upper_Burgundy "Upper Burgundy"). During the 6th or 7th century, the [Germanic speaking](/wiki/Alemannic_German "Alemannic German") [Alamanni](/wiki/Alamanni "Alamanni") moved into the area around Lake Biel, creating the language boundary that exists today. By the 8th century, the German-speaking population became the majority on the east end of the lake. In 999 [Rudolph III of Burgundy](/wiki/Rudolph_III_of_Burgundy "Rudolph III of Burgundy") granted lands around Lake Biel to the [Bishopric of Basel](/wiki/Bishopric_of_Basel "Bishopric of Basel"), during the formative period of the [Holy Roman Empire](/wiki/Holy_Roman_Empire "Holy Roman Empire"). Through the Bishop of Basel, the Counts of Neuchâtel and later the Counts of Neuchâtel-Nidau began to exercise their power in the foothills of the [Jura Mountains](/wiki/Jura_Mountains "Jura Mountains"). In 1140 the counts built [Nidau Castle](/wiki/Nidau_Castle "Nidau Castle") in the neighboring village of [Nidau](/wiki/Nidau "Nidau") to help secure their land on the eastern end of the lake. The town was probably built by the Bishop of Basel, Heinrich II von Thun, between 1225 (mention of *domum de Bilne*) and 1230 (mention of *in urbe mea de Beuna*). [Biel Castle](/w/index.php?title=Biel_Castle&action=edit&redlink=1 "Biel Castle (page does not exist)") was built either shortly before or shortly after the foundation of the town, to help support Nidau Castle.[[9]](#cite_note-HDS-9)

Officially, Biel remained under the jurisdiction of the [Bishop of Basel](/wiki/Bishop_of_Basel "Bishop of Basel") throughout the 11th to 18th centuries. However, the early history of the town is filled with conflict between the town council and the Bishop's representative. In 1252, the town council partly succeeded in becoming a [free imperial city](/wiki/Imperial_immediacy "Imperial immediacy"). In 1275 [King of Germany](/wiki/List_of_German_monarchs "List of German monarchs") [Rudolph of Habsburg](/wiki/Rudolph_I_of_Germany "Rudolph I of Germany") granted Biel a [town charter](/wiki/Town_charter "Town charter"). The town's legal position was strengthened in 1296 when Bishop Peter Reich von Reichenstein signed an agreement with the town. This original agreement was strengthened in 1352 and remained in force until 1798.

The town's church, the Church of St. Benedict, was first mentioned in 1228. The current church was built in 1451–70 and is regarded, after [Bern Cathedral](/wiki/Bern_Minster "Bern Minster"), as the second most important [late gothic](/wiki/Gothic_architecture "Gothic architecture") building in the Canton of Bern.[[9]](#cite_note-HDS-9)

### An associate of the Swiss Confederation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Biel/Bienne&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: An associate of the Swiss Confederation")]

While it officially remained part of the lands of the [Prince-Bishopric of Basel](/wiki/Prince-Bishopric_of_Basel "Prince-Bishopric of Basel"), starting in the 13th century Biel began making alliances with neighboring nobles and cities. In 1279 it allied with [Bern](/wiki/History_of_Bern "History of Bern"). This first alliance was followed in 1311 by an alliance with [Fribourg](/wiki/Fribourg "Fribourg"), a 1334 alliance with Solothurn, 1342 with [Murten](/wiki/Murten "Murten") and 1395 with [La Neuveville](/wiki/La_Neuveville "La Neuveville"). The alliance with Bern became an eternal alliance in 1352, as Bern itself joined the [Old Swiss Confederacy](/wiki/Old_Swiss_Confederacy "Old Swiss Confederacy"). Contradictory obligations to the Bishop of Basel, [Jean de Vienne](/wiki/Jean_de_Vienne_(archbishop,_died_1382) "Jean de Vienne (archbishop, died 1382)"), and to the [Imperial City of Bern](/wiki/Canton_of_Bern "Canton of Bern") led to a war in 1367. During the war, Biel was burned and the Bishop's castle was destroyed. After the extinction of the Counts of Neuchâtel-Nidau in 1375 the Bishop's power around the lake began to wane. In 1388, Bern gained control of Nidau Castle and the town of Nidau. However, the Bishop retained nominal power and influence in Biel. The two competing powers struggled for power in Biel for over 400 years and prevented the town from becoming completely independent from either powerful neighbor.[[9]](#cite_note-HDS-9)

Biel was considered an [associate](/wiki/Swiss_Associates "Swiss Associates") of the Swiss Confederacy during the 15th century, and after its participation in the [Burgundy Wars](/wiki/Burgundy_Wars "Burgundy Wars") even came to be recognized as a full member by 1494.

Even though Biel remained nominally under the control of the Catholic Bishops of Basel, in 1528 it converted to the new [Protestant](/wiki/Protestant_Reformation "Protestant Reformation") faith.

### From the French invasion to modern Biel/Bienne

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Biel/Bienne&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: From the French invasion to modern Biel/Bienne")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/72/Biel_von_Westen_1805.jpg/250px-Biel_von_Westen_1805.jpg)](/wiki/File:Biel_von_Westen_1805.jpg)

Biel/Bienne in 1805, while part of the First French Republic

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/79/Biel_Volkshaus_02.jpg/250px-Biel_Volkshaus_02.jpg)](/wiki/File:Biel_Volkshaus_02.jpg)

The Volkshaus/Maison du Peuple (People's House) is a symbol of the Social Democratic era of the town in the 1930s

The [French Revolution](/wiki/French_Revolution "French Revolution") changed the political situation in Biel/Bienne. In 1793, the [French Revolutionary Army](/wiki/Campaigns_of_1793_in_the_French_Revolutionary_Wars "Campaigns of 1793 in the French Revolutionary Wars") captured the Bishopric of Basel and brought the French into the lands near Biel. When they conquered the [Moutier](/wiki/Moutier "Moutier") valley and [Erguel](/wiki/Erguel "Erguel") in 1797 it brought the French practically to the gates of Biel/Bienne. On 6 February 1798, French troops marched through the open city gate while the population celebrated their arrival. Bienne and its neighboring communities were incorporated as the "Canton de Bienne" into the [département du Mont-Terrible](/wiki/Mont-Terrible "Mont-Terrible") of the [First French Republic](/wiki/First_French_Republic "First French Republic"). Two years later, in 1800, it went to the Département du [Haut-Rhin](/wiki/Haut-Rhin "Haut-Rhin"). Under Mayor Sigmund Wildermeth (1765–1847) Biel strictly followed every dictate from Paris.

After the collapse of the [French Empire](/wiki/First_French_Empire "First French Empire"), Biel sent Georg Friedrich Heilmann to the [Congress of Vienna](/wiki/Congress_of_Vienna "Congress of Vienna") in 1814 to push for the creation of an independent Canton of Biel. However, he was unsuccessful and the Congress granted most of the territory of the Bishopric to the canton of Bern. Biel was able to resist unification until Bern agreed to retain some of Biel's historic privileges and rights. In 1815 Biel finally joined the Canton of Bern as part of the Oberamt of Nidau. The city council of Biel struggled to make it the capital of its own district. Finally in 1832 the Biel Amtsbezirk was created and Biel became the district capital. The democratic reforms of the [Regeneration](/wiki/Restoration_and_Regeneration_(Switzerland) "Restoration and Regeneration (Switzerland)") era helped the citizens of Biel to identify with and feel a part of the Canton of Bern.[[9]](#cite_note-HDS-9)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d4/Plan_der_Stadt_Biel_1906_c.JPG/250px-Plan_der_Stadt_Biel_1906_c.JPG)](/wiki/File:Plan_der_Stadt_Biel_1906_c.JPG)

town map from 1906

By the beginning of the 20th century [anarcho-syndicalist](/wiki/Anarcho-syndicalist "Anarcho-syndicalist") groups, which saw strikes and sabotage as legitimate means to bring about reform, began to influence the labor movement in Biel/Bienne. The first large scale strike was the construction workers strike of 1902. The following years were marked with bitterly fought labor disputes. The largest strike was the journeymen carpenters strike of 1907, which lasted almost a year. Also in 1907 labor secretary Gottfried Reimann from the [Social Democratic Party](/wiki/Social_Democratic_Party_of_Switzerland "Social Democratic Party of Switzerland") was elected mayor. His election marked the first time that a Social Democrat was elected to such a powerful office in Switzerland.

The First World War meant a setback for the labor movement, even though Switzerland was not directly involved in the war. Wages were reduced significantly when the war started while inflation made everything more expensive. In July 1918, a demonstration of starving workers erupted into street riots that required military action to suppress.

In 1919 a Communist Party was founded in Biel, but it remained a minor party in the town. In 1921, the Social Democrats won a slim majority in the city councils. Under the leadership of the Social Democratic Mayor Guido Müller "Red Biel" began a series of socialist community experiments. During the 1930s the entire neighborhood around the train station was redeveloped according to the social planning theories of the era. The Volkshaus (People's House), built under the direction of Edward Lanz between 1928 and 1932, is an example of the "new building" style and a symbol of the Social Democratic era of the city.

In the years leading up to the Second World War, the Social Democrats began to lose power in the city. In the last year of the war, the [Swiss Party of Labour](/wiki/Swiss_Party_of_Labour "Swiss Party of Labour") gained nine seats on the city council and ended the Social Democrat majority. With the resignation of Mayor Müller in 1947, it would be almost thirty years (1976) before the Social Democrats had another mayor in Biel.[[9]](#cite_note-HDS-9)

On the occasion of the secession of the [canton of Jura](/wiki/Canton_of_Jura "Canton of Jura") in 1978, Biel had been asked to become its capital, but it remained with the canton of Bern.

The town was officially named *Biel* or **Bienne** until 2004, even though the bilingual *Biel-Bienne* was in common use. Since 2005, the official name has been *Biel/Bienne*, with [forward slash](/wiki/Slash_(punctuation) "Slash (punctuation)").[[10]](#cite_note-10)

At the beginning of the 20th century, the town's population was at 30,000 people. It doubled over the next 60 years, peaking at 65,000 in the mid-1960s. It declined gradually over the 1970s to 1990s, to below 49,000 in 2000, again rising slightly to just over 50,000 during the 2000s. Another 89,000 people live in the immediately surrounding [urban agglomeration](/wiki/Urban_agglomeration "Urban agglomeration").

## Geography and climate

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Biel/Bienne&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Geography and climate")]

### Topology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Biel/Bienne&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Topology")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bf/CH-Biel-Panorama.jpg/330px-CH-Biel-Panorama.jpg)](/wiki/File:CH-Biel-Panorama.jpg)

Lake Bienne with part of Biel/Bienne in the background

Biel/Bienne has an area of 21.19 km2 (8.18 sq mi).[[11]](#cite_note-Arealstatistik-11) Of this area, 1.7 km2 (0.66 mi2) or 8.0% is used for agricultural purposes, while 9.63 km2 (3.72 mi2) or 45.4% is forested. Of the rest of the land, 9.65 km2 (3.73 mi2) or 45.5% is settled (buildings or roads), 0.13 km2 (32 acres) or 0.6% is either rivers or lakes and 0.14 km2 (35 acres) or 0.7% is unproductive land.[[12]](#cite_note-BFS_land-12)

Of the built up area, industrial buildings made up 5.1% of the total area while housing and buildings made up 21.9% and transportation infrastructure made up 12.6%. Power and water infrastructure as well as other special developed areas made up 1.7% of the area while parks, green belts and sports fields made up 4.1%. Out of the forested land, all of the forested land area is covered with heavy forests. Of the agricultural land, 4.7% is used for growing crops and 2.0% is pastures. All the water in the municipality is flowing water.[[12]](#cite_note-BFS_land-12)

The municipality is at the southeastern foot of the [Jura Mountains](/wiki/Jura_Mountains "Jura Mountains") on the northeast end of [Lake Biel](/wiki/Lake_Biel "Lake Biel"). It consists of the village of Biel/Bienne, Vingelz (since 1900), Bözingen (since 1917), Madretsch and Mett (both since 1920).

On 31 December 2009 Amtsbezirk Biel, the municipality's former district, was dissolved. On the following day, 1 January 2010, it joined the newly created *Verwaltungskreis Biel/Bienne*. It remained the capital of the new Verwaltungskreis.[[6]](#cite_note-AGV-6)

### Government

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Biel/Bienne&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Government")]

The Municipal Council (fr: **Conseil municipal**, de: **Gemeinderat**) constitutes the [executive](/wiki/Executive_(government) "Executive (government)") government of the City of Biel/Bienne and operates as a [collegiate authority](/w/index.php?title=Collegiate_authority&action=edit&redlink=1 "Collegiate authority (page does not exist)"). It is composed of five councilors ([French](/wiki/French_language "French language"): *Conseiller municipal/ Conseillère municipale*, [German](/wiki/German_language "German language"): *Gemeinderat/ Gemeinderätin*), each presiding over a directorate. The president of the presidential directorate acts as [mayor](/wiki/Mayor "Mayor") (fr: *Maire*, de: *Stadtpräsident*). In the mandate period 2021–2024 (*législature*, *Legislatur*) the Municipal Council is presided by *Maire/ Stadtpräsident* [Erich Fehr](/w/index.php?title=Erich_Fehr&action=edit&redlink=1 "Erich Fehr (page does not exist)"). Departmental tasks, coordination measures and implementation of laws decreed by the City Council (parliament) are carried by the Municipal Council. The regular election of the Municipal Council by any inhabitant valid to vote is held every four years. Any resident of Biel/Bienne allowed to vote can be elected as a member of the Municipal Council. The current mandate period is from 1 January 2021 to 31 December 2024. The mayor is elected as such by public election by means of a system of [Majorz](/w/index.php?title=Majorz&action=edit&redlink=1 "Majorz (page does not exist)"), while the heads of the other directorates are assigned by the collegiate. The delegates are selected by means of a system of [Proporz](/wiki/Proportional_representation "Proportional representation").[[15]](#cite_note-15)

As of 2021[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Biel/Bienne&action=edit), Biel/Bienne's Municipal Council is made up of two representatives of the PS/SP ([Social Democratic Party](/wiki/Social_Democratic_Party_of_Switzerland "Social Democratic Party of Switzerland"), of whom one is also the mayor), one member of the *Grünes Bündnis (GB)* ([Green Party](/wiki/Green_Party_of_Switzerland "Green Party of Switzerland")), one of the PRR ([Les Radicaux Romands](/wiki/FDP.The_Liberals "FDP.The Liberals")), and one of the UDC/SVP ([Swiss People's Party](/wiki/Swiss_People%27s_Party "Swiss People's Party")), giving the left parties a majority of three out of five seats. The last regular election was held on 27 September 2020. The mayor has been reelected with 6889 votes (57.16%) and the voter turnout was 39.4%.[[16]](#cite_note-W2020-16)

The City Council (fr: **Conseil de ville**, de: **Stadtrat**), the city parliament, holds [legislative power](/wiki/Legislative_power "Legislative power"). It is made up of 60 members, with elections held every four years. The City Council decrees regulations and by-laws that are executed by the Municipal Council and the administration. The delegates are selected by means of a system of [proportional representation](/wiki/Proportional_representation "Proportional representation").

The sessions of the City Council are public. Unlike members of the Municipal Council, members of the City Council are not politicians by profession, and they are paid a fee based on their attendance. Any resident of Biel/Bienne allowed to vote can be elected as a member of the City Council. The Parliament holds its meetings in the *Stadtratssaal*.[[18]](#cite_note-20)

The last regular election of the City Council was held on 27 September 2020 for the mandate period (*la législature*) from 2021 to 2024. The voter turnout was 39.23%.[[16]](#cite_note-W2020-16)

Currently the City Council consist of 18 members of the [Social Democratic Party (PSR/SP)](/wiki/Social_Democratic_Party_of_Switzerland "Social Democratic Party of Switzerland") including 6 members of the French branch *Parti Socialiste Romand (PSR)* and 2 members of its junior parties *JUSO/JS*, 11 members of the [Liberals (PRR/FDP)](/wiki/FDP.The_Liberals "FDP.The Liberals") including 4 members of its French branch *Parti Radical Romand (PRR)*, 11 members of the [Swiss People's Party (UDC/SVP)](/wiki/Swiss_People%27s_Party "Swiss People's Party"), 8 members of the [Green Party (LV/Grüne)](/wiki/Green_Party_of_Switzerland "Green Party of Switzerland"), 4 [Green Liberal Party (PVL/GLP)](/wiki/Green_Liberal_Party_of_Switzerland "Green Liberal Party of Switzerland"), 2 members of the alliance called *Passarelle*, 2 members of the [Evangelical People's Party (PEV/EVP)](/wiki/Evangelical_People%27s_Party_of_Switzerland "Evangelical People's Party of Switzerland"), 2 members for the alliance of the two parties [Conservative Democratic Party (PBD/BDP) from Biel/Bienne (BLB)](/wiki/Conservative_Democratic_Party_of_Switzerland "Conservative Democratic Party of Switzerland") and [Christian Democratic People's Party (PDC/CVP)](/wiki/Christian_Democratic_People%27s_Party_of_Switzerland "Christian Democratic People's Party of Switzerland"), one member of the [Swiss Party of Labour (POP/PdA)](/wiki/Swiss_Party_of_Labour "Swiss Party of Labour"), and one member of the [Federal Democratic Union (UDF/EDU)](/wiki/Federal_Democratic_Union_of_Switzerland "Federal Democratic Union of Switzerland").[[16]](#cite_note-W2020-16)

## Demographics

### Population

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Biel/Bienne&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Population")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/13/Biel_Bahnhofquartier_01a.jpg/250px-Biel_Bahnhofquartier_01a.jpg)](/wiki/File:Biel_Bahnhofquartier_01a.jpg)

Apartments and street market near the train station

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ae/Biel_Moesli_2.JPG/250px-Biel_Moesli_2.JPG)](/wiki/File:Biel_Moesli_2.JPG)

Small apartments in the Mösliquartier/Petit-Marais

Biel/Bienne has a population (as of December 2020[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Biel/Bienne&action=edit)) of 55,206.[[22]](#cite_note-Stat2020_BE-24) As of 2010[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Biel/Bienne&action=edit), 28.1% of the population are resident foreign nationals. Over the last 10 years (2000–2010) the population has changed at a rate of 3.8%. Migration accounted for 7.8%, while births and deaths accounted for −1.4%.[[23]](#cite_note-SFSO-25)

Of the population in the municipality, 15,339 or about 31.5% were born in Biel/Bienne and lived there in 2000. There were 8,990 or 18.5% who were born in the same canton, while 9,170 or 18.8% were born somewhere else in Switzerland, and 12,244 or 25.2% were born outside of Switzerland.[[24]](#cite_note-STAT2000-26)

As of 2010[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Biel/Bienne&action=edit), children and teenagers (0–19 years old) make up 18.8% of the population, while adults (20–64 years old) make up 61.9% and seniors (over 64 years old) make up 19.3%.[[23]](#cite_note-SFSO-25)

As of 2000[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Biel/Bienne&action=edit), there were 19,980 people who were single and never married in the municipality. There were 21,188 married individuals, 3,727 widows or widowers and 3,760 individuals who are divorced.[[24]](#cite_note-STAT2000-26)

As of 2000[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Biel/Bienne&action=edit), there were 11,014 households that consist of only one person and 797 households with five or more people. In 2000[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Biel/Bienne&action=edit), a total of 23,367 apartments (86.8% of the total) were permanently occupied, while 2,169 apartments (8.1%) were seasonally occupied and 1,398 apartments (5.2%) were empty.[[25]](#cite_note-Housing-27) As of 2010[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Biel/Bienne&action=edit), the construction rate of new housing units was 3.2 new units per 1000 residents.[[23]](#cite_note-SFSO-25)

As of 2003[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Biel/Bienne&action=edit) the average price to rent an average apartment in Biel/Bienne was 935.83 [Swiss francs](/wiki/Swiss_franc "Swiss franc") (CHF) per month. The average rate for a one-room apartment was 463.73 CHF, a two-room apartment was about 706.49 CHF, a three-room apartment was about 846.98 CHF and a six or more room apartment cost an average of 1749.16 CHF. The average apartment price in Biel/Bienne was 83.9% of the national average of 1116 CHF.[[26]](#cite_note-BFS_rent-28) The vacancy rate for the municipality, in 2011[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Biel/Bienne&action=edit), was 2%.

### Language

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Biel/Bienne&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Language")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b4/2005-Biel-Quellgasse.jpg/250px-2005-Biel-Quellgasse.jpg)](/wiki/File:2005-Biel-Quellgasse.jpg)

Bilingual street sign

In 2000, a majority of the population spoke [German](/wiki/German_language "German language") (26,957 or 55.4%) as their first language. [French](/wiki/French_language "French language") was the second most common (13,695 or 28.1%) and [Italian](/wiki/Italian_language "Italian language") was third (2,925 or 6.0%). There were 37 people who spoke [Romansh](/wiki/Romansh_language "Romansh language").[[24]](#cite_note-STAT2000-26) German and French are both official languages of Biel/Bienne, which is the largest bilingual city in Switzerland.

In 2020, when asked specifically about the two official languages, 32,154 residents (56.8% of the population) mentioned German as their principal language, while 24,376 (43.2%) mentioned French.[[27]](#cite_note-29)

In recent years the city has used its linguistic assets as an economic advantage, becoming the Swiss *City of Communication*. Several call centres have been created in or around Biel, in addition to the traditional businesses established in the city and surrounding area, which have always exported most of their production worldwide.

### Religion

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Biel/Bienne&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Religion")]

According to the 2000 census[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Biel/Bienne&action=edit), 19,191 people or 39.4% of the total population, belonged to the [Swiss Reformed Church](/wiki/Swiss_Reformed_Church "Swiss Reformed Church"), while 14,241 or 29.3% were [Roman Catholic](/wiki/Roman_Catholic "Roman Catholic"). Of the rest of the population, there were 613 [members of an Orthodox church](/wiki/Eastern_Orthodox_Church "Eastern Orthodox Church") (or about 1.26% of the population), there were 87 individuals (or about 0.18% of the population) who belonged to the [Christian Catholic Church](/wiki/Christian_Catholic_Church_of_Switzerland "Christian Catholic Church of Switzerland"), and there were 2,870 individuals (or about 5.90% of the population) who belonged to another Christian church. There were 61 individuals (or about 0.13% of the population) who were [Jewish](/wiki/Judaism "Judaism"), and 3,156 (or about 6.49% of the population) who were [Muslim](/wiki/Muslim "Muslim"). There were 329 individuals who were [Buddhist](/wiki/Buddhism "Buddhism"), 235 individuals who were [Hindu](/wiki/Hinduism "Hinduism") and 68 individuals who belonged to another church. 6,012 (or about 12.36% of the population) belonged to no church, are [agnostic](/wiki/Agnosticism "Agnosticism") or [atheist](/wiki/Atheism "Atheism"), and 3,180 individuals (or about 6.54% of the population) did not answer the question.[[24]](#cite_note-STAT2000-26)

## Tourism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Biel/Bienne&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Tourism")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9c/Biel_-_Candino.jpg/250px-Biel_-_Candino.jpg)](/wiki/File:Biel_-_Candino.jpg)

Candino building in Biel/Bienne

Biel/Bienne is located near the watch-making cities of [La Chaux-de-Fonds](/wiki/La_Chaux-de-Fonds "La Chaux-de-Fonds") and [Le Locle](/wiki/Le_Locle "Le Locle"), which together form a UNESCO [World Heritage Site](/wiki/World_Heritage_Site "World Heritage Site"). The city is home to numerous watchmaking factories. The [Swatch Group](/wiki/Swatch_Group "Swatch Group") has its worldwide headquarters in the old [ASUAG](/wiki/ASUAG "ASUAG") building. The old city of Biel/Bienne includes a 15th-century Gothic church, guild halls, and fountains. Outside the old city, the Biel "Cultural Quarter" is home to the [Neuhaus](/w/index.php?title=Neues_Museum_Biel&action=edit&redlink=1 "Neues Museum Biel (page does not exist)") [[Wikidata](https://www.wikidata.org/wiki/Special:EntityPage/Q74516#sitelinks-wikipedia "d:Special:EntityPage/Q74516")] and Schwab Museums and the CentrePasquArt.

The [Jura mountains](/wiki/Jura_mountains "Jura mountains") are north of the town and two [funicular](/wiki/Funicular "Funicular") railways, the [Bienne–Evilard Funicular](/wiki/Bienne%E2%80%93Evilard_Funicular "Bienne–Evilard Funicular") and the [Biel–Magglingen Funicular](/wiki/Biel%E2%80%93Magglingen_Funicular "Biel–Magglingen Funicular"), link the city with the foothills. North-east of the town, the steep gorge of [Taubenloch](/wiki/Taubenloch "Taubenloch") is a popular place to visit. West of the city is [Lake Biel](/wiki/Lake_Biel "Lake Biel") which is lined with parks and the town's harbor.

In 2016 a total of 50,646 visitors spent 87,937 lodging nights in Biel/Bienne.[[28]](#cite_note-30)

### Heritage sites of national significance

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Biel/Bienne&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Heritage sites of national significance")]

The Alte Krone/La vieille Couronne, the artist's studio *Atelier Robert*, the former Rockhall [Manor](/wiki/Manor_house "Manor house"), the main train station, the Jordi-Kocher House, the [Catholic](/wiki/Roman_Catholic_Church "Roman Catholic Church") [parish](/wiki/Parish "Parish") Church of St. Maria Immaculata, the *[Kongresshaus](/w/index.php?title=Kongresshaus&action=edit&redlink=1 "Kongresshaus (page does not exist)")*/Palais des Congrès (Convention Center), the Kontrollgebäude at Zentralstrasse 49 / Oberer Quai 2, the *Neuhaus* Museum with the Robert Foundation Collection, the Schwab Museum, the [Swiss Reformed City Church](/wiki/City_Church_of_Biel "City Church of Biel"), the administration building and montage hall for General Motors, the Volkshaus Building and the *Waldleute Zunft* Building are listed as Swiss [heritage site of national significance](/wiki/Swiss_inventory_of_cultural_property_of_national_and_regional_significance "Swiss inventory of cultural property of national and regional significance"). The entire town of Biel/Bienne and the Taubenlochschlucht canyon are both part of the [Inventory of Swiss Heritage Sites](/wiki/List_of_cultural_property_of_national_significance_in_Switzerland:_Bern_A-M "List of cultural property of national significance in Switzerland: Bern A-M").[[29]](#cite_note-31)

### World Heritage Site

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Biel/Bienne&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: World Heritage Site")]

It is home to the [Vingelz / Hafen archaeological site](/w/index.php?title=Vingelz_/_Hafen_archaeological_site&action=edit&redlink=1 "Vingelz / Hafen archaeological site (page does not exist)") [[fr](https://fr.wikipedia.org/wiki/Biel-Vingelz%E2%80%93Hafen "fr:Biel-Vingelz–Hafen")]. Vingelz / Hafen is a prehistoric [pile-dwelling](/wiki/Stilt_house "Stilt house") (or stilt house) settlements that is part of the [Prehistoric Pile dwellings around the Alps](/wiki/Prehistoric_Pile_dwellings_around_the_Alps "Prehistoric Pile dwellings around the Alps") [UNESCO World Heritage Site](/wiki/UNESCO_World_Heritage_Site "UNESCO World Heritage Site").[[30]](#cite_note-UNESCO-32)

The Vingelz / Hafen site is buried under mud near the shore of Lake Biel. It is one of the best preserved sites on the lake and has had minimal research. Based on the limited studies done on the village, it was occupied around 2970–2820 BC and again in 2780–2695 BC. About 60 wood samples have been [dendrochronologically](/wiki/Dendrochronological "Dendrochronological") dated. The site was discovered in 1874 by Eduard von Fellenberg while he was excavating a [dug-out canoe](/wiki/Dug-out_canoe "Dug-out canoe"). In 1985 a series of test borings identified the two archaeological layers with a total thickness of about 90 cm (2 ft 11 in). A text excavation in 1998 found textile remains and a complete axe handle and blade.[[31]](#cite_note-33)

## Business

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Biel/Bienne&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Business")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bd/Rolex_Bienne_03.jpg/250px-Rolex_Bienne_03.jpg)](/wiki/File:Rolex_Bienne_03.jpg)

Rolex building in Biel/Bienne

The city and surrounding area are home to companies that design and manufacture specialised machinery and precision tools. Between 1936 and 1975 *General Motors Suisse SA* assembled over 300,000 [General Motors](/wiki/General_Motors "General Motors") automobiles here, mainly for the Swiss domestic market but also for export to neighboring countries and [Yugoslavia](/wiki/Socialist_Federal_Republic_of_Yugoslavia "Socialist Federal Republic of Yugoslavia").[[32]](#cite_note-GMCH-34) The city is also notable as a center of watchmaking:

* [Rolex](/wiki/Rolex "Rolex") movement and technical parts production
* [Swatch Group](/wiki/Swatch_Group "Swatch Group") has several of its brand headquarters here, especially [Omega SA](/wiki/Omega_SA "Omega SA") and Swatch.
* The [Federation of the Swiss Watch Industry FH](/wiki/Federation_of_the_Swiss_Watch_Industry_FH "Federation of the Swiss Watch Industry FH")
* [Glycine Watch SA](/wiki/Glycine_Watch_SA "Glycine Watch SA") manufacturing and administration
* [Doxa S.A.](/wiki/Doxa_S.A. "Doxa S.A.") headquarters

As of 2011, Biel/Bienne had an unemployment rate of 3.95%. As of 2008[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Biel/Bienne&action=edit), there were a total of 33,799 people employed in the municipality. Of these, there were 56 people employed in the [primary economic sector](/wiki/Primary_sector_of_the_economy "Primary sector of the economy") and about 10 businesses involved in this sector. 9,421 people were employed in the [secondary sector](/wiki/Secondary_sector_of_the_economy "Secondary sector of the economy") and there were 451 businesses in this sector. 24,322 people were employed in the [tertiary sector](/wiki/Tertiary_sector_of_the_economy "Tertiary sector of the economy"), with 2,267 businesses in this sector.[[23]](#cite_note-SFSO-25)

In 2008[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Biel/Bienne&action=edit) there were a total of 28,144 [full-time equivalent](/wiki/Full-time_equivalent "Full-time equivalent") jobs. The number of jobs in the primary sector was 43, of which 21 were in agriculture and 22 were in forestry or lumber production. The number of jobs in the secondary sector was 8,945 of which 7,405 or (82.8%) were in manufacturing and 1,388 (15.5%) were in construction. The number of jobs in the tertiary sector was 19,156. In the tertiary sector; 4,371 or 22.8% were in wholesale or retail sales or the repair of motor vehicles, 1,745 or 9.1% were in the movement and storage of goods, 1,092 or 5.7% were in a hotel or restaurant, 812 or 4.2% were in the information industry, 648 or 3.4% were the insurance or financial industry, 1,708 or 8.9% were technical professionals or scientists, 1,293 or 6.7% were in education and 3,591 or 18.7% were in health care.[[33]](#cite_note-35)

In 2000[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Biel/Bienne&action=edit), there were 17,680 workers who commuted into the municipality and 7,990 workers who commuted away. The municipality is a net importer of workers, with about 2.2 workers entering the municipality for every one leaving.[[34]](#cite_note-commuter-36) Of the working population, 31.2% used public transportation to get to work, and 37.8% used a private car.[[23]](#cite_note-SFSO-25)

## Education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Biel/Bienne&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Education")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0f/BFH_Biel%2C_Gebaeude_Solothurnstrasse_02_09.jpg/250px-BFH_Biel%2C_Gebaeude_Solothurnstrasse_02_09.jpg)](/wiki/File:BFH_Biel,_Gebaeude_Solothurnstrasse_02_09.jpg)

[Bern University of Applied Sciences](/wiki/Bern_University_of_Applied_Sciences "Bern University of Applied Sciences") (BFH) Bienne, building of the architecture, wood and civil engineering department, rue de Soleure in Bienne

In Biel/Bienne about 17,768 or (36.5%) of the population have completed non-mandatory [upper secondary education](/wiki/Education_in_Switzerland#Secondary "Education in Switzerland"), and 5,492 or (11.3%) have completed additional higher education (either [university](/wiki/List_of_universities_in_Switzerland "List of universities in Switzerland") or a *[Fachhochschule](/wiki/Fachhochschule "Fachhochschule")*). Of the 5,492 who completed tertiary schooling, 56.6% were Swiss men, 26.4% were Swiss women, 10.5% were non-Swiss men and 6.5% were non-Swiss women.[[24]](#cite_note-STAT2000-26)

The Canton of Bern school system provides one year of non-obligatory [Kindergarten](/wiki/Kindergarten "Kindergarten"), followed by six years of Primary school. This is followed by three years of obligatory lower Secondary school where the students are separated according to ability and aptitude. Following the lower Secondary students may attend additional schooling or they may enter an [apprenticeship](/wiki/Apprenticeship "Apprenticeship").[[35]](#cite_note-37)

During the 2009–10 school year, there were a total of 5,733 students attending classes in Biel/Bienne. There were 27 kindergarten classes with a total of 497 students in the municipality. Of the kindergarten students, 36.2% were permanent or temporary residents of Switzerland (not citizens) and 66.0% have a different mother language than the classroom language. The municipality had 79 primary classes and 1,470 students. Of the primary students, 32.9% were permanent or temporary residents of Switzerland (not citizens) and 53.4% have a different mother language than the classroom language. During the same year, there were 53 lower secondary classes with a total of 981 students. There were 23.6% who were permanent or temporary residents of Switzerland (not citizens) and 29.6% have a different mother language than the classroom language.[[36]](#cite_note-School-38)

As of 2000[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Biel/Bienne&action=edit), there were 3,008 students in Biel/Bienne who came from another municipality, while 517 residents attended schools outside the municipality.[[34]](#cite_note-commuter-36)

Biel/Bienne is home to 3 libraries. The Stadtbibliothek Biel, the *BFH Technik und Informatik TI Biel* and the *BFH Architektur, Holz und Bau AHB Biel*. There was a combined total (as of 2008[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Biel/Bienne&action=edit)) of 233,171 books or other media in the libraries, and in the same year a total of 501,646 items were loaned out.[[37]](#cite_note-39)

The newspapers *[Bieler Tagblatt](/wiki/Bieler_Tagblatt "Bieler Tagblatt")* and *[Journal du Jura](/wiki/Journal_du_Jura "Journal du Jura")* as well as the only totally bilingual German/French newspaper *Biel-Bienne* with its large free distribution within the greater area, are published in Biel.

## Transport

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Biel/Bienne&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Transport")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/83/Biel-Bienne_Bahnhof_Gare.jpg/250px-Biel-Bienne_Bahnhof_Gare.jpg)](/wiki/File:Biel-Bienne_Bahnhof_Gare.jpg)

Biel/Bienne's [central railway station](/wiki/Biel/Bienne_railway_station "Biel/Bienne railway station")

Biel/Bienne is very well connected to its region and to Switzerland as well.

The public transport in and around Biel/Bienne is operated by [Verkehrsbetriebe Biel/Transports publics biennois](/wiki/Verkehrsbetriebe_Biel/Transports_publics_biennois "Verkehrsbetriebe Biel/Transports publics biennois"), which is integrated into the fare network [libero](/wiki/Libero_(fare_network) "Libero (fare network)") with coordinated timetables, which in itself covers the area of [canton of Bern](/wiki/Canton_of_Bern "Canton of Bern") and [Solothurn](/wiki/Canton_of_Solothurn "Canton of Solothurn").

The fare network includes any mode of public transport, such as any kind of train (including the urban [S-Bahn](/wiki/Bern_S-Bahn "Bern S-Bahn")), [PostAuto buses](/wiki/PostBus_Switzerland "PostBus Switzerland"), [trams](/wiki/Trams_in_Bern "Trams in Bern"), buses (either [trolleybuses](/wiki/Trolleybuses_in_Bern "Trolleybuses in Bern") or motorized buses) and others. Fares are based on the number of zones crossed during a specified time and are independent of the mode of transport or the number of connections. Most part of Biel/Bienne and including [Nidau](/wiki/Nidau "Nidau") belong to fare zone **300**, including *Vingelz/Vigneules* in the southwest at the lake, but excluding *Hohfluh* on the Magglingen funicular and the *Bözigerfeld/Champs-de-Boujean* in the northeast, which belong to zone *301*.

The circle fare zone **301** around Biel/Bienne also includes [Tüscherz](/wiki/Twann-T%C3%BCscherz "Twann-Tüscherz") in the southwest, *Hohfluh*, [Evilard](/wiki/Evilard "Evilard"), and [Frinvillier](/wiki/Frinvillier "Frinvillier") ([German](/wiki/German_language "German language"): *Friedliswart*, through the *Taubenlochschlucht*) in the west, and [Orpund](/wiki/Orpund "Orpund"), [Scheuren](/wiki/Scheuren,_Switzerland "Scheuren, Switzerland"), [Schwadernau](/wiki/Schwadernau "Schwadernau"), [Brügg](/wiki/Br%C3%BCgg,_Bern "Brügg, Bern"), [Aegerten](/wiki/Aegerten "Aegerten"), and [Studen](/wiki/Studen,_Bern "Studen, Bern") in the east, and [Port](/wiki/Port,_Bern "Port, Bern"), [Ipsach](/wiki/Ipsach "Ipsach"), [Bellmund](/wiki/Bellmund "Bellmund"), [Jens](/wiki/Jens,_Switzerland "Jens, Switzerland"), [Merzligen](/wiki/Merzligen "Merzligen"), and [Hermrigen](/wiki/Hermrigen "Hermrigen") in the south of the municipality.

[Biel/Bienne railway station](/wiki/Biel/Bienne_railway_station "Biel/Bienne railway station") is not only the central network nucleus of Biel/Bienne, but also of the whole urban and inter-regional region. It connects the town to the regional, national and international railways network ([Neuchâtel](/wiki/Neuch%C3%A2tel "Neuchâtel") – [Lausanne](/wiki/Lausanne "Lausanne") - [Geneva](/wiki/Geneva "Geneva"), [La Chaux-de-Fonds](/wiki/La_Chaux-de-Fonds "La Chaux-de-Fonds"), [Bern](/wiki/Bern "Bern"), [Grenchen](/wiki/Grenchen "Grenchen") – [Delémont](/wiki/Del%C3%A9mont "Delémont") – [Basel](/wiki/Basel "Basel"), [Solothurn](/wiki/Solothurn "Solothurn") – [Olten](/wiki/Olten "Olten") – [Luzern](/wiki/Luzern "Luzern")/[Zürich](/wiki/Z%C3%BCrich "Zürich") – [St. Gallen](/wiki/St._Gallen "St. Gallen"), and the [canton of Jura](/wiki/Canton_of_Jura "Canton of Jura")). It is a central railway junction on the fast east–(south-)west line and on the Basel–Bern line. The station is Switzerland's thirteenth most busy railway station (about 52,0000 passengers per working day in 2016).

One [funicular railways](/wiki/Funicular_railway "Funicular railway") leads to the national sports center of [Magglingen/Macolin](/wiki/Magglingen "Magglingen") on the 500 metres (1,600 ft) higher Jura mountain in the west, and the other, the [Bienne-Evilard Funicular](/wiki/Bienne-Evilard_Funicular "Bienne-Evilard Funicular"), to the city hospital and to neighbouring municipality [Evilard](/wiki/Evilard "Evilard") to northwest, both above the town on the eastern range of the [Jura Mountains](/wiki/Jura_Mountains "Jura Mountains"). The high, flat pastures and wood of Magglingen/Macolin span about 7 kilometres (4.3 mi) from northeast to southwest at an altitude between 800 and 1,031 metres (2,625 and 3,383 ft). The [Magglingen/Macolin Funicular](/wiki/Magglingenbahn "Magglingenbahn") often leads to sunshine while Biel/Bienne is covered by low hanging clouds.

The port at the north-eastern end of [Lake Biel](/wiki/Lake_Biel "Lake Biel") is a starting point for leisurely journeys to the three lakes of Biel, [Neuchâtel](/wiki/Lake_Neuch%C3%A2tel "Lake Neuchâtel"), and [Murten/Morat](/wiki/Lake_Murten "Lake Murten") through the [Three-Lakes Landscape](/wiki/Seeland_(Switzerland) "Seeland (Switzerland)"), which are all connected by navigable channels and rivers. The port is situated on the west side of the main railway station between the exit of the river [Schüss/La Suze](/wiki/Suze_(river) "Suze (river)") arriving from the Jura in the west through the *Taubenlochschlucht* ([Swiss German](/wiki/Swiss_German "Swiss German") for pigeon hole gorge) and the navigable [Nidau-Büren Canal](/wiki/Nidau-B%C3%BCren_Canal "Nidau-Büren Canal") with connections as far as Solothurn.

Several bridges over the Nidau-Büren Canal connect the town to its south/eastern suburbs.

Biel/Bienne is well connected to other Swiss cities by several [motorways](/wiki/Autobahns_of_Switzerland "Autobahns of Switzerland") ([A6](/wiki/A6_(Switzerland) "A6 (Switzerland)") to Bern, and via [A5](/wiki/A5_(Switzerland) "A5 (Switzerland)") to both, the Jura and Basel, Luzern, Zurich, St. Gallen).

The town is very well connected to all Swiss international airports: [Geneva Airport](/wiki/Geneva_Airport "Geneva Airport") (1:40h), [EuroAirport Basel Mulhouse Freiburg](/wiki/EuroAirport_Basel_Mulhouse_Freiburg "EuroAirport Basel Mulhouse Freiburg") (1:30h), and [Zurich Airport](/wiki/Zurich_Airport "Zurich Airport") (1:30h), also serve as international gateways, all reachable within about the same time by direct [train](/wiki/SBB_CFF_FFS "SBB CFF FFS") from Biel/Bienne.
